blog-posts.title: The Resurgence of Old Vine Grenache
blog-posts.excerpt: Why winemakers are returning to ancient, low-yielding vines for the purest expression of terroir.

blocks.content:
Grenache is often misunderstood. For decades, it was the workhorse grape of the Mediterranean, producing high yields and high alcohol, but often lacking nuance.

Today, a new generation of winemakers is seeking out abandoned, century-old vineyards. These ancient, gnarled vines naturally restrict yields, producing incredibly concentrated, complex fruit. The resulting wines offer a haunting purity, with notes of wild strawberry, white pepper, and garrigue that transport you straight to the sun-baked hillsides of Southern France and Northern Spain.

When tasting these old-vine expressions, look for a balance between the grape's natural exuberance and the savory, earthy undertones brought forth by deep root systems.

blog-posts.title: Cellaring Basics: When to Open That Special Bottle
blog-posts.excerpt: A beginner's guide to temperature, humidity, and reading the structural evolution of fine wines.

blocks.content:
Deciding when to open a special bottle of wine can be agonizing. Open it too soon, and it might be tight and unyielding. Wait too long, and its vibrant fruit may have faded entirely.

The key to cellaring wine is consistent temperature (around 55°F or 13°C) and moderate humidity. But beyond storage conditions, understanding the wine's structure is paramount. Wines age well when they have a strong backbone of acidity, tannin, or in the case of sweet wines, sugar.

As wine ages, primary fruit flavors evolve into savory, tertiary notes like leather, tobacco, and dried mushrooms. The tannins soften and integrate, creating a smoother mouthfeel. If you have a case, open a bottle every few years to track its evolution. And remember: it is always better to open a wine five years too early than five minutes too late.

blog-posts.title: Decoding the Language of Tannins
blog-posts.excerpt: Understand how skins, seeds, and oak cooperate to give legendary wines their longevity.

blocks.content:
Tannins are an essential, yet often confusing, component of wine. They are naturally occurring polyphenols found in the skins, seeds, and stems of grapes, as well as in the oak barrels used for aging.

They create the drying sensation in your mouth—similar to drinking over-steeped black tea. But tannins are not just about texture; they act as a natural preservative, giving red wines the structural framework needed to endure decades in the cellar.

Not all tannins are created equal. 'Green' or unripe tannins can taste harsh and astringent. Conversely, 'ripe' tannins are often described as velvety, powdery, or silky, integrating beautifully with the wine's fruit. When tasting a highly tannic young wine, try pairing it with protein-rich foods like a nicely marbled steak, which will bind to the tannins and soften the wine on your palate.

about-content: Our events are curated for the students of Aalto University. Our priority is ensuring that students have access to learn about wine culture. We achieve this by subventing our tastings which makes them suitable for student budgets. In addition, we are primarily hosting our wine tastings on the campus of Aalto University to be physically accessible.

about-toggle-block-b-init-2-text:
Behind our every tasting is our exquisite board of student wine enthusiasts.

We hand select every wine, tend to create a suitable atmosphere and cater to our guests' needs so that they may focus on what is important – experiencing wine.
